---
title: "MLBLUE: Usage"
subtitle: "PyApprox Tutorial Library"
description: "Defining subsets, running MLBLUE end-to-end in PyApprox, comparing predicted and empirical variance against MLMC and GMF, and interpreting the optimal sample allocation across subsets."
tutorial_type: usage
topic: multi_fidelity
difficulty: intermediate
estimated_time: 10
prerequisites:
  - mlblue_concept
  - acv_many_models_usage
tags:
  - multi-fidelity
  - mlblue
  - api
  - estimator-comparison
  - sample-allocation
format:
  html:
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: true
    toc: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
jupyter: python3
---

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Download Notebook
[Download as Jupyter Notebook](notebooks/mlblue_usage.ipynb)
:::

## Learning Objectives

After completing this tutorial, you will be able to:

- Instantiate an MLBLUE estimator with any subset design using
  `MLBLUEEstimator(stat, costs, model_subsets=[...])`
- Run the standard pilot / allocate / generate / evaluate / call workflow for MLBLUE
- Read the optimised subset sample allocation and compare against other estimators
- Compare MLBLUE variance against MC, MLMC, and the CV oracle at multiple budgets
- Choose a subset design for a new problem


## Prerequisites

Complete [MLBLUE Concept](mlblue_concept.qmd) before this tutorial.
The [General ACV Usage](acv_usage.qmd) tutorial establishes the
pilot / allocate / generate / evaluate workflow that MLBLUE shares exactly.


## Quick Recap

MLBLUE solves the GLS normal equations
$\boldsymbol{\Psi}\mathbf{q}^B = \mathbf{y}$ to jointly estimate all model means.
The only structural input is the subset list — which models are co-evaluated.
PyApprox solves the semidefinite programme internally to find the optimal subset
sample counts.


## Setup

```{python}
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from pyapprox.util.backends.numpy import NumpyBkd
from pyapprox.benchmarks.instances.multifidelity.polynomial_ensemble import (
    polynomial_ensemble_5model,
)
from pyapprox.statest.statistics import MultiOutputMean
from pyapprox.statest.mc_estimator import MCEstimator
from pyapprox.statest.cv_estimator import CVEstimator
from pyapprox.statest.acv import MLMCEstimator
from pyapprox.statest.groupacv import (
    MLBLUEEstimator,
    MLBLUESPDAllocationOptimizer,
)

np.random.seed(1)
bkd = NumpyBkd()

benchmark = polynomial_ensemble_5model(bkd)
nmodels = 5
nqoi = benchmark.models()[0].nqoi()
costs = benchmark.costs()
variable = benchmark.prior()
models = benchmark.models()

costs_np = bkd.to_numpy(costs)
print("Benchmark: 5-model polynomial ensemble")
for i, c in enumerate(costs_np):
    print(f"  model {i}: relative cost = {c:.0e}")
```


## Stage 1: Pilot Study

```{python}
N_pilot = 500
s_pilot = variable.rvs(N_pilot)
vals_pilot = [m(s_pilot) for m in models]

stat = MultiOutputMean(nqoi, bkd)
(cov_pilot,) = stat.compute_pilot_quantities(vals_pilot)
stat.set_pilot_quantities(cov_pilot)

cov_np = bkd.to_numpy(cov_pilot)
print("Pilot pairwise HF-LF correlations:")
for a in range(1, nmodels):
    rho = cov_np[0, a] / np.sqrt(cov_np[0, 0] * cov_np[a, a])
    print(f"  rho(f0, f{a}) = {rho:.4f}")
```


## Stage 2: Define Subsets and Build the Estimator

The hierarchical subset design mirrors MLMC's level structure: start with all models,
then drop the cheapest one at a time. This is a strong default that works well for
model ensembles with a clear cost ordering.

```{python}
# Hierarchical subsets: all -> drop cheapest one at a time
subsets = [
    bkd.asarray([0, 1, 2, 3, 4], dtype=int),
    bkd.asarray([1, 2, 3, 4],    dtype=int),
    bkd.asarray([2, 3, 4],       dtype=int),
    bkd.asarray([3, 4],          dtype=int),
    bkd.asarray([4],             dtype=int),
]

target_cost = 1e2

est_mlblue = MLBLUEEstimator(stat, costs, model_subsets=subsets)
allocator = MLBLUESPDAllocationOptimizer(est_mlblue)
alloc_result = allocator.optimize(target_cost)
est_mlblue.set_allocation(alloc_result)

var_mlblue = float(bkd.to_numpy(est_mlblue.optimized_covariance())[0, 0])
print(f"MLBLUE predicted variance at P={target_cost:.0f}: {var_mlblue:.6f}")
print(f"Predicted std:                                    {var_mlblue**0.5:.5f}")
```


## Stage 3: Inspect the Optimal Subset Allocation

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Optimal MLBLUE subset sample allocation at three target costs ($P = 10, 100, 1000$). Each bar group is one target cost; bars within a group show the sample count for each model. The cheapest models always receive the most samples because information per unit cost is highest there."
#| label: fig-allocation

target_costs_show = [1e1, 1e2, 1e3]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, len(target_costs_show), figsize=(12, 4.5),
                         sharey=False)
colors = ["#2C7FB8", "#E67E22", "#27AE60", "#8E44AD", "#C0392B"]

for ax, P in zip(axes, target_costs_show):
    est = MLBLUEEstimator(stat, costs, model_subsets=subsets)
    alloc = MLBLUESPDAllocationOptimizer(est)
    result = alloc.optimize(float(P))
    est.set_allocation(result)

    nspm = bkd.to_numpy(est._nsamples_per_model)
    bars = ax.bar(range(nmodels), nspm, color=colors, edgecolor="k", lw=0.5)
    ax.set_xticks(range(nmodels))
    ax.set_xticklabels([f"$f_{i}$" for i in range(nmodels)])
    ax.set_title(f"P = {int(P)}", fontsize=11)
    ax.set_ylabel("Samples per model" if ax == axes[0] else "")
    ax.grid(True, alpha=0.2, axis="y")

    # Annotate bar values
    for bar, n in zip(bars, nspm):
        if n > 0:
            ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2, bar.get_height(),
                    f"{int(n)}", ha="center", va="bottom", fontsize=8)

fig.suptitle("MLBLUE: optimal sample allocation across budgets", fontsize=12)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Why cheap singletons dominate the allocation
At budget $P = 100$, the singleton $\{f_4\}$ costs $C_4 = 10^{-4}$ per sample.
That means $\sim 10^6$ singleton evaluations fit in the budget. Each singleton
evaluation contributes $C_4^{-1}$ information about $Q_4$, which through
$\boldsymbol{\Psi}^{-1}$ propagates to reduce uncertainty in $Q_0$.
The expensive all-models subset $\{f_0,\ldots,f_4\}$ contributes the critical
cross-model covariance information but can only be afforded in small quantities.
:::


## Stage 4: Compare Against Other Estimators

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Predicted estimator variance vs total cost on log-log axes. MLBLUE tracks closely toward the CV oracle floor, while MLMC plateaus at higher variance as the budget grows."
#| label: fig-comparison

costs_fine = np.logspace(1, 4, 15)

# CV oracle: known LF means
lowfi_stats = benchmark.ensemble_means()[1:, :]  # shape (nmodels-1, nstats)

estimator_configs = {
    "MC":     {"color": "#aaaaaa", "ls": ":", "lw": 1.5},
    "MLMC":   {"color": "#2C7FB8", "ls": "-", "lw": 2},
    "MLBLUE": {"color": "#27AE60", "ls": "--", "lw": 2.5},
    "CV oracle": {"color": "#C0392B", "ls": ":", "lw": 1.5},
}

sweep_results = {name: [] for name in estimator_configs}

for P in costs_fine:
    P = float(P)

    est = MCEstimator(stat, costs[:1])
    est.allocate_samples(P)
    sweep_results["MC"].append(
        float(bkd.to_numpy(est.optimized_covariance())[0, 0]))

    est = MLMCEstimator(stat, costs)
    est.allocate_samples(P)
    sweep_results["MLMC"].append(
        float(bkd.to_numpy(est.optimized_covariance())[0, 0]))

    est = MLBLUEEstimator(stat, costs, model_subsets=subsets)
    alloc = MLBLUESPDAllocationOptimizer(est)
    result = alloc.optimize(P)
    est.set_allocation(result)
    sweep_results["MLBLUE"].append(
        float(bkd.to_numpy(est.optimized_covariance())[0, 0]))

    est = CVEstimator(stat, costs, lowfi_stats=lowfi_stats)
    est.allocate_samples(P)
    sweep_results["CV oracle"].append(
        float(bkd.to_numpy(est.optimized_covariance())[0, 0]))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))
for name, cfg in estimator_configs.items():
    vals = sweep_results[name]
    ax.loglog(costs_fine, vals, cfg["ls"], color=cfg["color"],
              lw=cfg["lw"], label=name)

ax.set_xlabel("Total cost $P$", fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel("Predicted estimator variance", fontsize=12)
ax.set_title("MLBLUE vs other estimators -- variance vs budget", fontsize=11)
ax.legend(fontsize=10)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.2, which="both")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
# Print variance ratios at selected costs
target_costs_cmp = [1e1, 1e2, 1e3]
est_names = ["MC", "MLMC", "MLBLUE", "CV oracle"]

print(f"\n{'Estimator':<14}", end="")
for P in target_costs_cmp:
    print(f"  P={int(P):>5}", end="")
print("\n" + "-" * (14 + len(target_costs_cmp) * 9))

# Collect variances: dict[name][cost_idx]
all_vars = {n: [] for n in est_names}
for P in target_costs_cmp:
    P = float(P)

    est_mc = MCEstimator(stat, costs[:1])
    est_mc.allocate_samples(P)
    all_vars["MC"].append(float(bkd.to_numpy(est_mc.optimized_covariance())[0, 0]))

    est = MLMCEstimator(stat, costs)
    est.allocate_samples(P)
    all_vars["MLMC"].append(float(bkd.to_numpy(est.optimized_covariance())[0, 0]))

    est = MLBLUEEstimator(stat, costs, model_subsets=subsets)
    alloc = MLBLUESPDAllocationOptimizer(est)
    result = alloc.optimize(P)
    est.set_allocation(result)
    all_vars["MLBLUE"].append(float(bkd.to_numpy(est.optimized_covariance())[0, 0]))

    est = CVEstimator(stat, costs, lowfi_stats=lowfi_stats)
    est.allocate_samples(P)
    all_vars["CV oracle"].append(float(bkd.to_numpy(est.optimized_covariance())[0, 0]))

for name in est_names:
    print(f"{name:<14}", end="")
    for j in range(len(target_costs_cmp)):
        print(f"  {all_vars[name][j]/all_vars['MC'][j]:>7.4f}", end="")
    print()
```


## Stage 5: Empirical Verification

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Distributions of MLBLUE (green) and MLMC (blue) HF mean estimates from 200 independent trials at equal total cost $P = 100$. Both are centred on the true mean (red dashed). MLBLUE achieves a tighter distribution, confirming the predicted variance reduction."
#| label: fig-distributions

# Allocate MLBLUE at target_cost = 100
est_mlblue = MLBLUEEstimator(stat, costs, model_subsets=subsets)
alloc = MLBLUESPDAllocationOptimizer(est_mlblue)
result = alloc.optimize(target_cost)
est_mlblue.set_allocation(result)

est_mlmc_emp = MLMCEstimator(stat, costs)
est_mlmc_emp.allocate_samples(target_cost)

n_trials = 200
true_mean = float(bkd.to_numpy(benchmark.ensemble_means())[0, 0])

def run_trials(est, n_trials, seed_offset=0):
    vals = []
    for i in range(n_trials):
        np.random.seed(i + seed_offset)
        spm = est.generate_samples_per_model(variable.rvs)
        vpm = [models[a](spm[a]) for a in range(len(spm))]
        vals.append(float(est(vpm)))
    return np.array(vals)

mlblue_trials = run_trials(est_mlblue,   n_trials, seed_offset=7000)
mlmc_trials   = run_trials(est_mlmc_emp, n_trials, seed_offset=8000)

all_vals = np.concatenate([mlblue_trials, mlmc_trials])
xpad = 0.08 * all_vals.std()
xlim = (all_vals.min() - xpad, all_vals.max() + xpad)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4.5), sharey=True)
for ax, trials, name, col in zip(
    axes,
    [mlblue_trials, mlmc_trials],
    ["MLBLUE", "MLMC"],
    ["#27AE60", "#2C7FB8"],
):
    ax.hist(trials, bins=40, density=True, color=col, alpha=0.80,
            edgecolor="k", lw=0.2)
    ax.axvline(true_mean, color="#C0392B", ls="--", lw=2, label="True mean")
    ax.set_xlabel(r"Estimate of $\mu_0$", fontsize=11)
    ax.set_title(f"{name}   std = {trials.std():.4f}", fontsize=11)
    ax.set_xlim(xlim)
    ax.legend(fontsize=9)
    ax.grid(True, alpha=0.2)
axes[0].set_ylabel("Density", fontsize=11)

fig.suptitle(f"MLBLUE vs MLMC  |  P={int(target_cost)}  |  {n_trials} trials",
             fontsize=12)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(f"True mean:     {true_mean:.5f}")
for name, tr, est_obj in [
    ("MLBLUE", mlblue_trials, est_mlblue),
    ("MLMC", mlmc_trials, est_mlmc_emp),
]:
    pred_std = float(bkd.to_numpy(est_obj.optimized_covariance())[0, 0])**0.5
    print(f"{name}:  mean = {tr.mean():.5f}  std = {tr.std():.5f}  "
          f"(predicted std = {pred_std:.5f})")
```


## Alternative Subset Designs

```{python}
# Design A: pairwise -- HF co-evaluated with each LF model individually
subsets_star = [
    bkd.asarray([0, 1], dtype=int),
    bkd.asarray([0, 2], dtype=int),
    bkd.asarray([0, 3], dtype=int),
    bkd.asarray([0, 4], dtype=int),
    bkd.asarray([4],    dtype=int),   # cheap singleton to gather LF info
]

# Design B: budget-aware -- skip subsets with highest-cost model unless small
subsets_budget = [
    bkd.asarray([0, 1, 2], dtype=int),   # HF + two medium LF
    bkd.asarray([2, 3, 4], dtype=int),   # three cheap LF
    bkd.asarray([3, 4],    dtype=int),
    bkd.asarray([4],       dtype=int),
]

designs = {
    "Hierarchical (default)": subsets,
    "Pairwise (star)":        subsets_star,
    "Budget-aware":           subsets_budget,
}

mc_est_ref = MCEstimator(stat, costs[:1])
mc_est_ref.allocate_samples(target_cost)
mc_var_ref = float(bkd.to_numpy(mc_est_ref.optimized_covariance())[0, 0])

print(f"Subset design comparison at P = {int(target_cost)}:\n")
print(f"{'Design':<28}  {'Predicted var':>14}  {'Ratio vs MC':>12}")
print("-" * 58)
for name, subs in designs.items():
    est_d = MLBLUEEstimator(stat, costs, model_subsets=subs)
    alloc_d = MLBLUESPDAllocationOptimizer(est_d)
    result_d = alloc_d.optimize(target_cost)
    est_d.set_allocation(result_d)
    var_d = float(bkd.to_numpy(est_d.optimized_covariance())[0, 0])
    print(f"{name:<28}  {var_d:>14.6f}  {var_d/mc_var_ref:>12.4f}")
```

## Common Errors

**All-singleton subsets.** If every subset is a singleton, $\boldsymbol{\Psi}$ is
diagonal and MLBLUE reduces to $S+1$ independent per-model mean estimates with no
variance reduction for $Q_0$. At least one subset must contain the HF model alongside
at least one LF model.

**Missing model coverage.** If any model appears in no active subset,
$\boldsymbol{\Psi}$ is singular and allocation will fail. Every
model referenced in the subsets list must be covered.

**Stale allocation.** Calling `stat.set_pilot_quantities` with new data does not
refresh the allocation. Always call `MLBLUESPDAllocationOptimizer.optimize(P)` again
after any pilot update.

**`generate_samples_per_model` output.** The method returns one array per model,
with each array having the correct number of samples for that model.
Pass the full returned list unchanged to the estimator call.


## Key Takeaways

- Instantiate MLBLUE with `MLBLUEEstimator(stat, costs, model_subsets=[...])`
  where each element is a `bkd.asarray(...)` of model indices
- Allocate with `MLBLUESPDAllocationOptimizer(est).optimize(target_cost)`, then
  `est.set_allocation(result)`
- The hierarchical subset design performs comparably to best GMF (PACV) on most
  problems; the right design depends on computational feasibility
- The cheapest subsets always dominate the allocation — information per unit cost
  is highest there


## Next Steps

- [Pilot Studies Concept](pilot_studies_concept.qmd) — How to size the pilot study
  to obtain reliable covariance estimates for all subset pairs
- [Ensemble Selection Concept](ensemble_selection_concept.qmd) — Given the MLBLUE
  framework, which models to include in the ensemble in the first place


## References

- [SUSIAMUQ2020] D. Schaden, E. Ullmann. *On multilevel best linear unbiased estimators.*
  SIAM/ASA J. Uncertainty Quantification 8(2):601-635, 2020.
  [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1137/19M1263534)

- [SUSIAMUQ2021] D. Schaden, E. Ullmann. *Asymptotic Analysis of Multilevel Best Linear
  Unbiased Estimators.* SIAM/ASA J. Uncertainty Quantification 9(3):953-978, 2021.
  [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1137/20M1321607)

- [CWARXIV2023] M. Croci, K. Willcox, S. Wright. *Multi-output multilevel best linear
  unbiased estimators via semidefinite programming.* Computer Methods in Applied
  Mechanics and Engineering, 2023.
  [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cma.2023.116130)
